Home › TRT & Metabolic Health

TRT & Metabolic Health

Low testosterone and poor metabolic health aren't separate problems. They're the same problem, showing up in different ways.

Men with low testosterone are more likely to carry excess body fat — particularly visceral fat (the kind stored around organs). That visceral fat, in turn, suppresses testosterone production. Blood sugar regulation becomes harder. Energy drops. Exercise feels harder, so activity levels fall. The cycle compounds.

Most TRT clinics treat the hormone side in isolation. Ted's Health is different: we have a registered dietitian working alongside our doctors, specifically because the metabolic and hormonal sides of men's health are inseparable for many of our patients.

2.4x
Higher risk of low T in men with metabolic syndrome
40%
Of men over 45 experience low testosterone levels
50%
Of men with type 2 diabetes have low testosterone
3-6
Months for TRT to show meaningful body composition changes

The cycle that keeps men stuck

How low testosterone and poor metabolic health reinforce each other

1
Testosterone declinesNatural ageing, stress, poor sleep, or underlying health conditions cause T levels to drop.
2
Energy and motivation fallLess energy makes exercise harder. Motivation to cook well drops. Patterns that supported metabolic health start to slip.
3
Visceral fat accumulatesWith lower activity and disrupted metabolism, visceral fat increases — particularly around the abdomen.
4
Aromatase converts testosterone to oestrogenVisceral fat contains the enzyme aromatase, which converts testosterone into oestrogen. The more fat, the faster the conversion.
5
Insulin resistance developsLow testosterone is linked to poorer insulin sensitivity — making weight management harder and increasing type 2 diabetes risk.
6
Testosterone falls furtherThe cumulative effect suppresses testosterone production further — deepening every symptom and making the cycle self-sustaining.

Breaking this cycle requires addressing both sides. TRT restores the hormonal baseline. Dietary intervention addresses the metabolic driver that was suppressing testosterone in the first place. Together, results are significantly better than either approach alone.

What our approach looks like

Your Ted's Health journey typically starts with a blood test — measuring testosterone, alongside metabolic markers like fasting glucose, HbA1c, and lipid panel. The results give our clinical team a full picture: not just your hormone levels, but the metabolic context around them.

If TRT is appropriate, your doctor leads on prescribing and monitoring. If metabolic health is a factor — which it often is — you can also work with Mei Wan, our registered dietitian, on a dietary plan that complements your treatment.

This integrated model is uncommon in UK TRT. Most clinics prescribe and monitor. Ted's Health goes further: we address the whole system.

Common questions

Can TRT help with weight loss?

TRT can support fat loss — particularly visceral (belly) fat — by improving insulin sensitivity and energy levels, which makes exercise more effective. However, TRT isn't a weight loss treatment on its own. The best outcomes come from combining TRT with dietary changes, which is why Ted's Health offers both.

Is there a link between low testosterone and type 2 diabetes?

Yes, and the relationship runs in both directions. Low testosterone is associated with insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome. Men with type 2 diabetes have lower average testosterone levels than men without. Addressing low testosterone can improve insulin sensitivity and blood sugar control.

Do I need to be on TRT to work with your dietitian?

No. Mei works with men across the spectrum — those considering TRT, those already on TRT, and those who want to address metabolic health through diet alone. A dietitian consultation doesn't require a TRT prescription.

How does visceral fat affect testosterone?

Visceral fat — the fat stored around your organs — contains an enzyme called aromatase, which converts testosterone into oestrogen. The more visceral fat you carry, the faster this conversion happens, which lowers free testosterone levels. This is why weight loss and TRT often work in tandem.

What does metabolic health actually mean?

Metabolic health refers to how efficiently your body processes energy — how well it regulates blood sugar, blood pressure, cholesterol, and body composition. Poor metabolic health (metabolic syndrome) and low testosterone frequently appear together, and improving one tends to improve the other.

Start with a blood test

We measure testosterone alongside your metabolic markers — giving you and your clinician the full picture.

Order a blood test Meet Mei Wan

Ted's Health is a CQC-registered provider of men's health treatments in the UK. All treatments are UK-licensed and prescribed by registered clinicians. The statistical figures on this page are derived from published clinical research. This page is for informational purposes and does not constitute medical advice. For personalised guidance, please consult one of our doctors.
